title: Trump's $87B War Bill Meets Congressional Revolt, IAEA vs Tehran on Inspections, and a Continent-Sized Heatwave
subhead: A day of friction across the board: Trump demands $87B for the Iran war but faces bipartisan resistance, Iran pushes back on nuclear inspections despite a signed deal, Europe fries under record heat, and Ukraine blacks out Crimea.

sections.heading: Trump Requests $87.6B for Iran War, Congress Pushes Back
sections.body:
The White House formally asked Congress to approve $87.6 billion, with the bulk — $67 billion — earmarked for the Defense Department under 'Operation Epic Fury,' the US military campaign against Iran. The request includes $21 billion for munitions, $17.3 billion for operational costs, and $12.1 billion for classified programmes. According to the BBC, the Pentagon's chief financial officer told Congress last month the war had already cost roughly $29 billion, though defence analysts and lawmakers say that estimate does not reflect the full financial toll.

The request landed one day after the Republican-controlled Senate passed a resolution rebuking Trump's war powers — the first resolution of its kind to clear Congress. Trump responded by calling the four Republican senators who voted with Democrats 'losers' and held a tense closed-door luncheon with Senate Republicans where Senator Bill Cassidy of Louisiana said he confronted the president directly: 'You have not told the American people what's going on.' Cassidy told reporters the war was 'supposed to last four weeks, it's lasted four months,' and that the original objectives had not been achieved.

The supplemental request also includes $11 billion for US farmers and $1.4 billion for the Ebola response in Central Africa — making the package harder to oppose outright but also raising questions about whether domestic and humanitarian spending was bundled with war funding to force passage.
sections.whyItMatters: The $87.6B request is the first full accounting of the Iran war's cost trajectory, and the Senate war-powers vote signals that even Republican support for the conflict is eroding. How this plays out will shape both the war's continuation and the midterm landscape.

sections.heading: IAEA Chief Says Iran Inspections Will Happen; Tehran Disagrees
sections.body:
IAEA Director General Rafael Grossi told reporters in Japan that nuclear inspections in Iran 'will indeed take place' under the preliminary US-Iran peace agreement signed last week. Grossi said the 14-point memorandum of understanding states 'explicitly' that the dilution of Iran's highly enriched uranium would be carried out under IAEA supervision, and that the agency was 'working on modalities — dates, procedures, places — very soon.'

But Iran's Deputy Foreign Minister Kazem Gharibabadi pushed back hard on X, writing that access to Iran's damaged nuclear facilities and nuclear material would 'only be addressed within the framework of a final agreement with the US' and after 'practical steps' to lift all sanctions were taken. 'Media noise cannot be used to impose facts on the ground,' he wrote.

The gap matters. Before the war, the IAEA reported Iran had 440 kg of uranium enriched to 60% purity — near weapons grade, theoretically enough for as many as 10 bombs. The agency was allowed to visit the Bushehr nuclear power plant earlier this month, but has still not been granted access to the sites bombed during the 12-day US-Israeli campaign. Without that access, the IAEA cannot verify the current size, composition, or whereabouts of Iran's enriched uranium stockpile, much of which is believed to be in underground tunnels at Isfahan.
sections.whyItMatters: The entire verification architecture of the ceasefire deal hinges on IAEA access. Tehran's insistence that inspections wait for a final agreement — which has a 60-day negotiation window — means the most sensitive sites could remain opaque for months.

sections.heading: Europe's Record Heatwave: 43C in France, Red Alerts Across the Continent
sections.body:
Western Europe is under an unprecedented heatwave. France recorded its hottest day since records began in 1947, with the national temperature indicator hitting 30C (averaging day and night temperatures across dozens of locations). Temperatures reached 43C in the Poitou-Charentes region. More than half the country remains under a red heat alert. Tens of thousands of homes in the west lost power. The Louvre and the Eiffel Tower both closed early; a Louvre spokesperson said the building was 'not sufficiently adapted to climate change.'

The UK saw its hottest June day on record — 36.1C in Gosport, Hampshire — with forecasters warning 38C was possible on Thursday. Spain recorded its highest-ever daily average temperature for June (28.17C on Tuesday). Italy has 16 red heat alerts in northern and central regions. Germany, the Netherlands, and Belgium expect peak temperatures later in the week. France's labour minister, Jean-Pierre Farandou, said the country is 'in the process of finding out we've become a hot country.'

The heatwave is now spreading eastward, with severe warnings for Poland, Croatia, and Hungary. Several drownings were reported in Germany, and drought warnings are in effect across multiple German states. Europe is the fastest-warming continent, heating at twice the global average according to the Copernicus climate service.
sections.whyItMatters: This is not a one-off event. Europe's heating rate is double the global average, and infrastructure — housing, transit, power grids — was built for a different climate. The simultaneous power outages and cultural landmarks shutting down illustrate the compounding failures that heatwaves now trigger routinely.

sections.heading: Ukraine Blacks Out Sevastopol as E5 Leaders Prepare for NATO Summit
sections.body:
Ukrainian drone strikes knocked out power in Sevastopol, the largest city in Russian-occupied Crimea, overnight. Moscow-installed governor Mikhail Razvozhayev said some areas would be without electricity until Wednesday evening and urged residents to conserve phone battery. Ukraine's Unmanned Systems Forces commander, Robert Brovdi, said 48 military sites were targeted, including Sevastopol's main power substation. Explosions were also reported near Kerch and a Russian aerospace forces base at Mount Ai-Petri.

The strike comes amid growing fuel shortages and panic-buying in Crimea, as Ukraine intensifies efforts to isolate the peninsula. Russia's defence ministry claimed it destroyed over 300 Ukrainian drones overnight; Kyiv said 95 of 101 Russian drones targeting Ukraine were intercepted.

Meanwhile in Berlin, the E5 group — Germany, Britain, France, Italy, and Poland — pledged 'to further substantially support Ukraine' including through sanctions and energy-sector resilience. German Chancellor Friedrich Merz said the message to Russia was: 'Ukraine remains strong.' The leaders also discussed a European military mission in the Strait of Hormuz, conditional on the US-Iran deal being finalised. All five leaders will attend the NATO summit in Ankara on July 7-8 alongside Trump.
sections.whyItMatters: Ukraine's strike on Crimea's power grid shows it can still impose significant costs on Russian logistics. The E5's statement, ahead of the Ankara summit, is a signal to both Moscow and Washington that European support is not wavering.

sections.heading: Venezuela Hit by Twin Major Earthquakes, Buildings Collapse in Caracas
sections.body:
Venezuela was struck by back-to-back powerful earthquakes on Wednesday. According to the US Geological Survey, a magnitude 7.2 quake hit about 160 km west of Caracas, followed less than a minute later by a magnitude 7.5 tremor — the largest to hit Venezuela in more than a century, according to the New York Times. Buildings collapsed in Caracas. Reuters reported that rescue operations were underway and 'high casualties' were likely.

The twin quakes struck a country already deep in economic and political crisis. Venezuela's infrastructure — buildings, hospitals, emergency services — has been severely degraded by years of hyperinflation, emigration, and governance failures. The scale of the disaster will strain whatever response capacity remains.
sections.whyItMatters: A 7.5-magnitude earthquake hitting a country with collapsed infrastructure is a catastrophe multiplier. The death toll is likely to be significant, and Venezuela's capacity to respond without international assistance is minimal.

sections.heading: Colombia's Right Turn and Zimbabwe's Constitutional Power Grab
sections.body:
In Latin America, Colombia's left-wing presidential candidate Iván Cepeda conceded defeat, confirming right-wing businessman Abelardo de la Espriella as president-elect. De la Espriella won by less than a percentage point — the narrowest margin in recent Colombian history — but has already moved to realign the country internationally. He accepted an invitation for Colombia to join the US-led 'Shield of the Americas' alliance against drug cartels and pledged to restore diplomatic ties with Israel, which outgoing President Gustavo Petro severed in 2024 over the Gaza war. Trump had publicly backed de la Espriella and labelled Cepeda a 'radical Left Marxist.'

In Zimbabwe, the Senate voted 75-4 to approve a constitutional amendment extending presidential terms from five to seven years and replacing direct presidential elections with election by parliament. The amendment would keep President Emmerson Mnangagwa, 83, in office until 2030. Opponents have called it a 'constitutional coup.' Human Rights Watch has documented intimidation and violence against those who opposed the changes. The bill now awaits Mnangagwa's own signature.
sections.whyItMatters: Two different models of democratic erosion on the same day. In Colombia, a razor-thin election driven partly by foreign interference concerns produces a sharp foreign-policy reversal. In Zimbabwe, a ruling party that has held power since 1980 simply rewrites the constitution to remove the electorate from the equation.

bottomLine: June 25 was a day of friction on nearly every front. Trump's $87.6B Iran war request puts a price tag on a conflict Congress is increasingly unwilling to back. Iran is publicly disputing the inspection terms of its own ceasefire deal, making verification the next fault line. Europe is literally on fire — record temperatures are closing landmarks and knocking out power grids built for a cooler era. Ukraine demonstrated it can still impose costs on Russia by blacking out Sevastopol, while European leaders aligned ahead of next month's NATO summit. A 7.5-magnitude earthquake struck a Venezuela already hollowed out by crisis. And in Colombia and Zimbabwe, democracy moved in opposite directions — one narrowly, one by constitutional rewrite. The common thread: old arrangements are under strain everywhere, and the institutions meant to manage them are being tested simultaneously.
